dos: Cool the burn with running cool (not cold) water for 10–20 minutes
Cover loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth or cling wrap
Remove rings or tight items near the burn before swelling starts
Give paracetamol for pain if available
donts: Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or oil on burns
Do NOT burst blisters
Do NOT remove clothing stuck to the burn
Do NOT use fluffy cotton wool directly on the wound
urgent: Seek immediate care for burns larger than your palm, burns on face/hands/genitals, or burns that are deep and painless (nerve damage).

dos: Apply firm, direct pressure with a clean cloth
Keep pressing — do not remove cloth (add more on top if soaked)
Elevate the injured area above the heart if possible
Keep the person still and warm
donts: Do NOT remove the cloth to check the wound
Do NOT use a tourniquet unless trained and only for limbs with life-threatening bleeding
Do NOT give food or water if surgery may be needed
urgent: All serious bleeding requires clinic or hospital care urgently. Call for help immediately.

dos: Encourage them to cough forcefully if they can
Give up to 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades
If back blows fail: give up to 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver)
For infants: use gentle back taps and chest thrusts only
donts: Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants under 1 year
Do NOT do blind finger sweeps in the mouth
Do NOT give water or food
urgent: If the airway is not cleared after back blows and thrusts, call for emergency help immediately.

dos: Lay the person flat on their back
Raise their legs 30 cm above heart level if possible
Loosen tight clothing around neck and waist
Fan them and speak calmly when they regain consciousness
donts: Do NOT give water or food until fully conscious
Do NOT leave them alone
Do NOT sit them up too quickly
urgent: Seek care if the person does not regain consciousness within 1–2 minutes, or if they have chest pain, difficulty breathing, or seizures.

dos: Keep the person still and calm — movement spreads venom faster
Immobilize the bitten limb at or below heart level
Remove watches, rings, or tight clothing near the bite
Get to a hospital or clinic as fast as possible with antivenom
donts: Do NOT cut the wound or try to suck out venom
Do NOT apply ice or tourniquet
Do NOT give alcohol or food
Do NOT try to catch the snake
urgent: ALL snake bites should be treated as medical emergencies. Reach a health facility with antivenom immediately.

dos: Give Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) in small sips — frequently
If no ORS: mix 1 liter water + 6 teaspoons sugar + ½ teaspoon salt
Keep giving fluids even if person vomits — small amounts often
For infants: continue breastfeeding plus ORS
donts: Do NOT give only plain water in severe cases (lacks salts)
Do NOT give sugary sodas or juice as replacement
Do NOT ignore dehydration in children — it worsens fast
urgent: Seek clinic care urgently if the person cannot keep fluids down, is unconscious, has sunken fontanelle (infants), or has no urine output for 8+ hours.

signs: Sudden loss of consciousness with convulsions
Confusion or drowsiness after the episode
dos: Time the seizure — note when it starts
Protect the head with soft padding underneath
Gently turn the person on their side (recovery position) after convulsions stop
Stay with them until fully alert
donts: Do NOT restrain the person or hold them down
Do NOT put anything in their mouth
Do NOT give water or food during or immediately after
urgent: Call for help if: seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, person doesn't regain consciousness, this is a first-ever seizure, or they are injured.

dos: Move them to shade or a cool environment immediately
Cool the body rapidly — wet cloths on neck, armpits, groin
Fan the person vigorously while applying cool water
Give cool water if they are conscious and can swallow
donts: Do NOT give aspirin or paracetamol (won't help heat stroke)
Do NOT leave the person in the sun or hot space
Do NOT give fluids if unconscious — risk of choking
urgent: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency. Seek hospital care immediately while cooling the person.
